author: nflaig
content:
in the fork choice spec we have
```python
 parent_block = store.blocks[block.parent_root]
 bid = block.body.signed_execution_payload_bid.message
```
this breaks if parent is pre-gloas, does fork choice just not care about this? it seems like gloas is the first time this is relevant

author: potuz
content: I realized that there's a minimal change that allows to keep all of consensus without changing the checkpoint structure and it allows us to justify full and thus have finalization boundary aligned. It's just a minimal change to process justification and finalization. I'll have a PR ready today to show as an alternative

author: nflaig
content:
>  I think we don't even need execution_requests in the ExecutionPayloadEnvelope anymore but that would require engine_newPayload to return them instead
ah realized this is necessary for optimistic sync

author: potuz
content: Nevermind this, we wouldn't be able to count attestations for unknown branches 😦

author: potuz
content: yeah this was noticed before and I assume all clients case this, the problem is that the spec is fork-specific, including fork awareness in the helpers is ugly.

author: nflaig
content: https://github.com/ethereum/consensus-specs/pull/5094 (unless I used some evil spec sorcery it seems possible)

author: potuz
content: can you tl;dr me instead of reviewing the code first? how do you sync 33 as asked above without the payload of 31? how do you verify the requests?

author: potuz
content: do you commit in the bid?

author: potuz
content: like here?

author: nflaig
content: you have the commitment as part of the finalized state, I don't see why you even need the payload

author: potuz
content: yeah that's why I asked if you commit in the bid

author: nflaig
content: and I think with that change you can even optimistically sync the chain without requiring the `ExecutionPayloadEnvelope`

author: potuz
content: you need the hash validation if we want to keep the same assurances as today

author: potuz
content: left a comment, I think you need to enforce when processing the payload that the requests match the commitment in the bid, otherwise there's a chain deadlock, since no proposer can build on full.  The payload needs to be marked as invalid

author: tbenr
content: I like the approach and I believe it is a big improvement.

author: nflaig
content: agree, I have that check, but it might be in the wrong place

author: potuz
content: I'll take a stab at implementing the changes, I *suspect* it'll make our caching harder as mentioned above, but not clear that it's a blocker for us

author: potuz
content: oh I only checked in `process_execution_payload`

author: potuz
content: oh yeah you have it on the forkchoice handler

author: nflaig
content: you said that before, but for lodestar it removes a lot of complexity in our state cache, essentially what we have on mainnet now

author: nflaig
content: so I am curious why that is for prysm, having to worry about 2 states per slot per root seem just more complex to me

author: potuz
content: I think it's on the NSC, but I may be wrong, will find out when I code it, it doesn't look too bad

author: potuz
content: so the only real dissadvantage is the cost of making the beacon block larger by the requests. Do we know how large these can be?

author: potuz
content: these get now twice being broadcast in full

author: nflaig
content: I was wondering if we can remove them from `ExecutionPayloadEnvelope`, I think this was just added so you can run the state transition without consulting the EL?

author: potuz
content: the problem of not having them in the envelope is what I say above that forkchoice will enforce that you build on full

author: nflaig
content: maybe pandas could help? the are most of the time empty it seems but their theoretical ssz size limit is high

author: potuz
content: and attesters will not be able to take any incoming block as head

author: potuz
content: so attesters need to have the requests available when the payload arrives to give builder safety

author: nflaig
content: yeah you right i think, also optimistic sync needs it for sure

author: nflaig
content: well we do sent them as part of the header right now which is very time sensitive, see https://github.com/ethereum/builder-specs/issues/120, but this is more theoretical, I don't think they are large in practice but also haven't looked at data

author: potuz
content: So one worry I can see right now is that the builder could cause an ex-ante reorg by sending valid requests to the majority of the chain, invalid ones to a minority and the proposer being among the minority. But these sort of issues already exist today I believe on payload equivocation.  So we need to make sure that PTC votes on the valid requests being there.

author: tbenr
content: I see gossip rules to reject non-matching requests root with the bid. should it be enough? committed but invalid requests are noop correct?

author: potuz
content: Yeah in principle this prevents the payload from propagating over the chain, and if the proposer gets the invalid one first, it will attempt to reorg it whikle the people that got the valid one first will take it as valid, these sort of ex-ante reorgs are already in the current forkchoice so this does not make the situation worse I think. They aren't as bad as the current ex-ante reorg cause they won't steal a payload.

author: potuz
content: <@795439202732867594> could you weigh in the impact of broadcasting the requests twice and adding them to the beacon block?

author: tbenr
content: it seems the double send of the request is unavoidable. so we are exchanging bandwidth with single state transition (with all the good consequences of it). I believe it is significantly net-positive.

author: tbenr
content: and maybe we could remove this duplication in the future, on the other side, once the double-state concept lends on clients, the complexity\bugs damage would be done 🙂

author: potuz
content: yeah I think the removal of the handling of empty-full states from db/stategen is a big win

author: potuz
content: we should allocate time to see if it affects forkchoice or cosensus in any way

author: tbenr
content: I have the feeling that this is orthogonal to FC though. If a node has a state attached or not should be irrelevant

author: potuz
content: yeah, but for example offhchain issues like the above if there are equivocations and attesters need to enforce the payload being full, etc.

author: tbenr
content:
Ye but the verifiability of the payload seems unaffected. 

Can you recall me why requests need to be blinded in the bid?

author: tbenr
content: Just efficiency?

author: potuz
content: you are thinking on keeping them full in the bid  and avoid rebroadcasting? I think this is ugly in the event of invalid payloads but also yes, sending them full in the bid is a problem.

author: tbenr
content: Ye i was thinking that. It has free da issues at least

author: nflaig
content: aren't there even complains from builders that having them in the bid leaks information about the payload?

author: tbenr
content: i'm not an expert but the requests from EL to CL don't have much to leak

author: nflaig
content:
ah it was noted here https://github.com/ethereum/builder-specs/issues/120
> There also has been concerns (eg. #108) about leaking block information via the logs bloom.
but I am not sure if this means same as logs blooms or similar

author: bharath.vedartham
content: today as per fulu, we are sending the full execution requests along with the blinded payload in the BuilderBid as per here

author: bharath.vedartham
content: https://github.com/ethereum/builder-specs/blob/main/specs/electra/builder.md?plain=1#L36

author: bharath.vedartham
content: In terms of MEV leaking, I believe the payload is the critical part and not the requests. otherwise I m pretty confident builders would have raised issues against the execution requests being fully in the bid

author: tbenr
content: seems like an efficiency argument, mostly

author: nflaig
content: that is just because we need them for stf, not because we want to include them per se

author: nflaig
content: so gloas removes a lot of potential information leakage

author: bharath.vedartham
content: yeah by just having the block hash in the bid, in terms of leakage i think its better than what we have today in the `BuilderBid` which is the full execution payload header

author: bharath.vedartham
content: fair my point is that, i dont think having the execution requests in the bid should contribute to any info leak in the bid

author: potuz
content: So if we don't optimize to preexecute  the requests, our cache actually becomes simpler. If the requests aren't that much of computation this actually simplifies things for us as well

author: tbenr
content:
speaking of which.

current design doesn't have requests written onchain in beacon blocks, while the new design has.
If requests are not affecting payload validity by design, is the new design open to big free DA issues?

author: potuz
content: Having them in the bid would give free DA but in the block how so?

author: tbenr
content: oh this is similar to what we have today

author: potuz
content: Today the builder can include whatever it wants

author: potuz
content: Anyway

author: tbenr
content: no no, talking about unblinded requests in beaconblock

author: potuz
content: As long as the Payload is valid I don't see any change from today

author: tbenr
content: ye exactly

author: potuz
content: <@539495253418180618> <@592004585506340885> could we schedule a breakout to discuss the above before interop? Do we have enough people that are informed of the change to make a decision or at least to put us in a place so that we decide immediately in the next meeting/interop? The change is really minor from a consensus perspective and it does simplify implementation. There's a trade-off on bandwidth and perhaps some complications on proposing (requests need to be cashed and recovered in case of reorgs). We started analyzing internally

author: potuz
content: It may delay devnet 2 but not devnet 1 IMO so it can be decided in parallel to testing

author: potuz
content: The reorg situation is one of the complications I could find. It puts us back into what we had for withdrawals that we ended up caching in the state to avoid bugs. But this time is not so bad cause the envelope should have them and we save those in DB

author: jtraglia
content: Is the breakout portion of ACDT not sufficient for this?

author: potuz
content: I think so if people come informed and actually come on Monday. But the timing typically sucks for lighthouse

author: jtraglia
content: The timing will always suck for someone 😢

author: jtraglia
content: I agree, people should come prepared.
